title: Hosea 12:2–14
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: The Pattern of the Prophecy
points: Hosea prophesied for over 50 years (760–710 BC) to the northern kingdom of Israel, also called Ephraim
The theme of Hosea is the relentless pursuit of an unfaithful people by an absolutely faithful God
Gomer’s physical adultery to Hosea illustrates Israel’s spiritual adultery: total abandonment or syncretism with the Baals
Chapter 12 follows the same pattern: indictment of unfaithfulness, announcement of consequences, call to repentance
Three unique emphases in chapter 12: Jacob as a repeated illustration, the undoing of the Exodus, and God’s repeated speech through prophets

outline.title: Truth 1: God Is Justified in Punishing Anyone Because of Their Sins (vv. 2, 14)
points: God brings a charge against both Judah and Israel (Jacob), indicting both kingdoms as guilty of spiritual adultery
The Assyrian captivity of 720 BC is the major act of judgment foreseen throughout the prophecy
God’s punishment is not cruelty but justice: Israel will dwell in tents again, reversing the blessings of the land (v. 9)
The objection ‘a God of love would not do that’ reflects making ourselves the standard rather than God
What should amaze us is not that God judges but that not all perish; God in his goodness reaches out and turns people to himself

outline.title: Truth 2: God’s Grace Shows Itself in Calling His People to Repentance (vv. 3–6, 13)
points: Jacob is used three times as a positive illustration: his birth (grasping the heel), his wrestling with God, and his exile to Syria
In Genesis 32, Jacob wrestled all night with the angel of the LORD and refused to let go until he received a blessing—his hip was dislocated yet he prevailed
The point: Israel should be wrestling with God like its forefather, not chasing the wind of false gods (v. 1)
Hosea says God ‘spoke to us’ through Jacob (v. 4)—the Old Testament scriptures were written for our learning and admonition (Romans 15:4; 1 Corinthians 10:11)
God’s call to repentance in verse 6: ‘Return, observe mercy and justice, and wait on your God continually’—repentance accompanied by its fruit

outline.title: Truth 3: Resisting God’s Grace Ends in Separation from God (vv. 7–8, 14)
points: Instead of repenting, Ephraim behaves like a cunning Canaanite with dishonest scales (v. 7)—acting just like the world
Ephraim self-justifies: ‘I have found wealth for myself; they shall find in me no iniquity’ (v. 8)—prosperity taken as divine approval
The problem began when Israel refused to purge the land of false altars; they thought worldly allegiances could coexist with faithfulness to God
Colossians 2:8 warns against being taken captive by empty philosophy and worldly tradition: you are complete in Christ and need nothing the world offers
God withdraws his grace from those who persistently resist it—allowing them to have what the desires of their hearts crave

outline.title: Truth 4: Even in Judgment There Is Mercy (vv. 12–13)
points: Jacob fled to Syria as a fugitive from his brother, worked for Laban for a wife, and tended sheep in exile
Yet even in that difficult exile, God was present: he provided Jacob a family, and through Jacob’s line the Lord Jesus Christ ultimately came
God remains Immanuel even when he disciplines his people; he never ceases to be God-with-us in times of trial
Romans 8:35–39: nothing can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus—trials are there to bring us to know that love
The warning of verse 14 stands: if we provoke him to anger, God steps back and allows us to bear the full penalty of our sins without an alien atonement

summary:
Returning to the ongoing series on the prophecy of Hosea, Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from Hosea 12:2–14, a chapter that follows the familiar pattern of Hosea’s prophecy—indictment, consequences, call to repentance—while adding several distinctive emphases. The pastor begins by recapping the book: Hosea prophesied for over 50 years to the northern kingdom, illustrating Israel’s spiritual adultery through Hosea and Gomer’s real marriage; the major act of judgment foreseen is the Assyrian captivity of 720 BC. Chapter 12 uniquely employs Jacob as a threefold positive illustration, highlights the undoing of the Exodus as God’s judgment, and underscores God’s repeated speaking through the prophets while Israel refused to listen.

The sermon draws out four truths. First, God is entirely justified in punishing Israel for her sins. The objection that a God of love would not judge reveals that we have made ourselves the standard for God’s behavior and have underestimated the wickedness of sin. Second, God’s grace is manifest precisely in his persistent call to repentance: Jacob’s birth, his all-night wrestling match with the angel of the LORD (Genesis 32), and his exile in Syria are held up as encouragements—Israel should be wrestling with God like their forefather rather than chasing the emptiness of idols. Third, resisting God’s grace leads inevitably to separation from God. Ephraim has become a cunning Canaanite, justifying its sins by pointing to its prosperity, and has adopted the world’s philosophies rather than purging them—a warning applied to the contemporary church through Colossians 2:8. Fourth, even in judgment there is mercy: God provided Jacob a family even in his Syrian exile, and through Jacob’s line came the Lord Jesus Christ. Nothing separates God’s people from his love—but those who provoke him most bitterly will be left to bear the full penalty of their sins without an alien atonement.
transcript:
If you're able to remain standing for the reading of God's Word, we're reading from Hosea chapter 12, as we return to our series on the prophecy of Hosea. Hosea chapter 12, starting at verse 2. This is the Word of our Lord.

> *"The Lord also brings a charge against Judah, and will punish Jacob according to his ways; according to his deeds He will recompense him. He took his brother by the heel in the womb, and in his strength he struggled with God. Yes, he struggled with the Angel and prevailed; he wept and sought favor from Him. He found him in Bethel, and there He spoke to us — that is, the Lord God of hosts. The Lord is His memorable name. So you, by the help of your God, return; observe mercy and justice, and wait on your God continually.*

> *A cunning Canaanite! Deceitful scales are in his hand; he loves to oppress. And Ephraim said, 'Surely I have become rich, I have found wealth for myself; in all my labors they shall find in me no iniquity that is sin.' But I am the Lord your God ever since the land of Egypt; I will again make you dwell in tents, as in the days of the appointed feast. I have also spoken by the prophets, and have multiplied visions; I have given symbols through the witness of the prophets. Though Gilead has idols — surely they are vanity — though they sacrifice bulls in Gilgal, indeed their altars shall be heaps in the furrows of the field. Jacob fled to the country of Syria; Israel served for a spouse, and for a wife he tended sheep. By a prophet the Lord brought Israel out of Egypt, and by a prophet he was preserved. Ephraim provoked Him to anger most bitterly; therefore his Lord will leave the guilt of his bloodshed upon him, and return his reproach upon him."* (Hosea 12:2–14)

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray. Father, we pray that You would open our eyes to see glorious things concerning You from this portion of Your inspired and inerrant Word. We pray that Your Spirit will be working in a mighty way, that we might fall in love with Jesus even as we hear about the condemnations of those who have forsaken You. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen. Please be seated.

If you are a parent of a child older than an infant, you have experienced the frustration of telling your kids the same thing over and over and over again with no apparent positive response. Or if you have a loved one who suffers from dementia, you know what it is to repeat yourself again and again, knowing you are going to have to do it again and again and again — because what you are saying is important and because you love them.

That is exactly what we see going on here in Hosea — and not just in Hosea but throughout the Old Testament prophets. We find God repeating himself again and again, and we see the people of God not listening again and again — and yet God repeats himself because he loves those who are his.

It has been a while since we were in Hosea, so let me briefly remind us of a few things concerning this prophecy. Hosea prophesied for over 50 years — from approximately 760 BC to 710 BC — to the northern kingdom. After Israel divided into two kingdoms, the ten northern tribes became a kingdom to themselves, often called Israel, and sometimes called Ephraim because Ephraim was the largest tribe among them.

The theme of Hosea is the relentless pursuit of an unfaithful people by an absolutely faithful God. Israel's spiritual adultery is illustrated from the beginning of the book by Gomer's physical adultery against Hosea. Hosea and Gomer were real people — not a parable or illustration — who lived 3,000 years ago and went through everything this book describes. That marriage illustrates Israel's spiritual adultery committed with the gods of the land.

Israel did this in two ways. Some people completely abandoned God — they wanted nothing to do with him and simply served the Baals, the masters of the land. But the majority did not abandon God completely. They thought it wise to syncretize — to mix what the gods of the land offered with what God said, and come up with a religion that pleased both. In both cases they were unfaithful to the Lord. And the major act of God's judgment in this prophecy is the Assyrian captivity of 720 BC.

When we come to chapter 12, God follows the same pattern we have seen throughout: he points out how his people have been unfaithful, he points out the consequences, and he calls them to repent. In that sense chapter 12 is more of the same. And yet there are some unique things. Three times Jacob is used as a positive illustration: "Look at your forefather — he wrestled with me for a blessing, while you are wrestling with false gods. Follow his example." We also find what had been hinted in earlier chapters made very clear here: in judging his people by sending them into captivity, God is *undoing the Exodus*. The Exodus is the major redemptive act of the Old Testament; the cross is the major redemptive act of the New. And God says: because of your unfaithfulness, I am removing you from the land and putting you back into captivity — the undoing of the Exodus. A third emphasis here is that God has spoken to them repeatedly. He spoke to them in Bethel (v. 4), through the prophets (v. 10), and through Hosea himself — "the Lord speaks," while their dumb idols do not.

I want to draw four truths from this chapter.

## First: God Is Justified in Punishing Anyone Because of Their Sins

Look at verse 2: "The Lord also brings a charge against Judah, and will punish Jacob according to his ways; according to his deeds He will recompense him." And then verse 14: "Ephraim provoked Him to anger most bitterly; therefore his Lord will leave the guilt of his bloodshed upon him, and return his reproach upon him."

Though Hosea was primarily a prophecy to the northern kingdom, he also speaks to Judah, the southern kingdom. By this point both kingdoms stand indicted before the Lord — both have committed spiritual adultery by worshiping the gods of the land. And we read passages like this and we think God is cruel, mean. "A God of love would not do that." What we are actually saying is: "A God of love would not do that because I would not do that — and since I am the standard for what God should do, God should not do that either." We also think this because we do not grasp the heinousness of sin, the wickedness of rebellion against God.

Whatever happens to Israel is just, because they sinned against God. Brothers and sisters, what should amaze us is not that all did not perish — the expectation should be that every last one who sins against God would perish. And those of you who are not trusting Christ: you will perish unless you repent and turn to God. That should be all of our fates. And yet God in his goodness reaches out and turns us to himself so that we do not all perish but have everlasting life.

God says he will again make Israel dwell in tents as in the days of the appointed feast (v. 9) — not for a week as in the Feast of Tabernacles, but in exile. God is removing the blessings that come from faithfulness to him. This is not God's meanness; it is God's justice against a people who refused to respond to his goodness. And God says it is going to happen in the captivity — all because they did not want to repent.

God has been faithful to Israel since the Exodus. His presence was known through the Tabernacle, through the priestly and prophetic offices — he has daily met with Israel for hundreds of years. And yet the people want something else. So in verse 14: "Ephraim provoked Him to anger most bitterly." God is completely justified in letting Israel reap what it has sown in rebellion.

He does the same today. If you are in rebellion against God, he may allow you to reap what you are sowing. One of the worst things that can happen to anyone, especially to an unbeliever, is for God to let you have the desires of your heart. As you continue to rebel against God, he will give you over to your debased mind and allow you to continue separating from him — because that is what you are saying you want. But if you abandon God, do not expect the blessings that come from living life under God to continue.

## Second: God's Grace Shows Itself in Calling His People to Repentance

That is where Jacob comes in. In verses 3–4 and verse 12, there are three examples from Jacob's life: his birth (he was born grasping his twin brother's heel), his wrestling match with God (Genesis 32), and his self-exile in Syria to live with Laban (Genesis 28–31).

Bible scholars are divided as to whether Hosea refers to Jacob positively or negatively — is he an example to follow or an example of what not to do? We take the view that this is a positive example: Jacob is an encouragement to Israel.

What we see is this: Israel, like Jacob, should be struggling with God instead of chasing the wind. In verse 1 Hosea says Israel is chasing the wind — trying to grab the wind. A former president of Brazil once said in a press conference that wind power was wonderful because you could store the wind in big bags and on windless days just open the bags. That is how stupid it is to chase what cannot be grabbed — and yet that is exactly what Israel was doing instead of wrestling with God.

How often do we do that in our lives? Chasing the meaningless, the empty, the futile — what cannot be grabbed — instead of wrestling with God in our hearts, in prayer, in his Word. We are so quick to condemn the Old Testament saints for their sins while being hypocrites in our condemnation, because we also chase the wind instead of wrestling with God.

Jacob was born second, but even from the womb he pursued God's blessing. In Genesis 32, coming back from Syria with his family, fearing that Esau was going to kill him, Jacob crossed the brook Jabbok — and a man wrestled with him all night. Eventually the man dislocated Jacob's hip, which would have been excruciating, hoping Jacob would give up. And yet he would not. To the point that the angel said: "Let Me go, for the day breaks." And Jacob said: "I will not let You go unless You bless me!" And the angel blessed him. That is the point: Israel, like Jacob, should be struggling with God for blessing while you are chasing the emptiness of false gods.

And notice in verse 4 that Hosea says that in Jacob's wrestling with God, "He spoke to us" — not "to Jacob," but "to us." Hosea is foreshadowing what Paul later tells us in Romans 15:4: "For whatever things were written before were written for our learning, that we through the patience and comfort of the Scriptures might have hope." And 1 Corinthians 10:11: "Now all these things happened to them as examples, and they were written for our admonition, upon whom the ends of the ages have come." Brothers and sisters, Jacob spoke to the Israel of Hosea's time. The Bible — all of it — speaks to us today. Hosea is pointing Israel to the Scriptures because the Scriptures point them to their God.

And what is God saying to them in verse 5? That God is the LORD, the covenantal God of Israel, the God who brought Israel out of Egypt. It is this God who calls them to repentance in verse 6: "So you, by the help of your God, return; observe mercy and justice, and wait on your God continually."

I wonder why the people who criticize God for being mean do not point out that every time God pronounces judgment, he also says: there is a way out. Just repent. Turn to me. Trust me. Recognize me as the God who is your God and who blesses you. God says: you do that by my help. I am here to help you. God graciously puts Jacob before his people and says: he was just like you, and yet he followed me. You can do the same.

Micah, a contemporary of Hosea, issues the same calling in Micah 6:8: "He has shown you, O man, what is good; and what does the Lord require of you but to do justly, to love mercy, and to walk humbly with your God?"

If you profess faith in Christ but are currently living in rebellion to your Lord, look to those in your life who are pointing you to Christ. See the Christ they are calling you to see. Turn from your sins to the God who has promised never to leave you nor forsake you.

## Third: Resisting God's Grace Leads to Separation from God

Verse 14: "Ephraim provoked Him to anger most bitterly; therefore his Lord will leave the guilt of his bloodshed upon him, and return his reproach upon him."

Instead of turning to the Lord, Ephraim rejoices in its sin. Verses 7–8: "A cunning Canaanite! Deceitful scales are in his hand; he loves to oppress. And Ephraim said, 'Surely I have become rich, I have found wealth for myself; in all my labors they shall find in me no iniquity that is sin.'"

The prophet is accusing the Israelites of behaving just like the people of the land. "Cunning Canaanite" — if you have an ESV, it may say "merchant," but the word is simply "Canaan." The point of the prophet: you are behaving just like the pagans around you. And Ephraim says: so what? It made me wealthy. God doesn't think this is sin — otherwise, why would I be prospering?

I find it interesting that God uses dishonest business practices to condemn Israel. It almost sounds like the Al Capone situation — he was eventually jailed not for all his violent crimes but for tax evasion. That was the only case they could make stick. Is that what God is doing here? No — the point is that even in seemingly minor things like fudging scales, they are acting like the world, and that is what brings condemnation from God.

This began when God's people did not obey his command to purge the land of everything that was anti-God. They were supposed to eliminate all the altars of the false gods, all the false gods themselves — but they didn't. They said: it is okay, we can live with the world and be friends with the world. We can benefit from the philosophies of the world.

Brothers and sisters, we often think we can keep some things the world offers while still being faithful to Christ, when the Bible says otherwise. Paul says in Colossians 2:8: "Beware lest anyone cheat you through philosophy and empty deceit, according to the tradition of men, according to the basic principles of the world, and not according to Christ. For in Him dwells all the fullness of the Godhead bodily, and you are complete in Him." Hear that — you are complete in Christ. You do not need any worldly idea to complete you. You do not need any worldly wisdom to tell you how to become like Christ, how to deal with anxiety or depression or any other struggle. Christ is sufficient. The Word of Christ is sufficient. Don't be like the Israelites — cunning Canaanites rather than the people who follow the Lord.

## Fourth: Even in Judgment, There Is Mercy

Look at verses 12–13: "Jacob fled to the country of Syria; Israel served for a spouse, and for a wife he tended sheep. By a prophet the Lord brought Israel out of Egypt, and by a prophet he was preserved."

Jacob went into his own exile in Syria — a fugitive from his brother, having to work for his uncle Laban. And yet even in the land away from home, even in being a fugitive, even having to work hard — God was still present with Jacob. How do we know? God provided Jacob a family. And through Jacob's family ultimately came the Lord Jesus Christ.

God remains Immanuel even when he disciplines us. He never ceases being God-with-us, even in times of trial, even in discipline, even in great frustration and difficulty. Nothing separates God's people from his love. And that love will sometimes put us through difficult things so that we know God is the only one worth loving — so that we will not fall after idols. He uses trials to keep us close to him.

Paul says in Romans 8:35–39:

> *"Who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Shall tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or peril, or sword? As it is written, 'For Your sake we are killed all day long; we are accounted as sheep for the slaughter.' Yet in all these things we are more than conquerors through Him who loved us. For I am persuaded that neither death nor life, nor angels nor principalities nor powers, nor things present nor things to come, nor height nor depth, nor any other created thing, shall be able to separate us from the love of God which is in Christ Jesus our Lord."*

Nothing will separate us — not even your trials. Your trials are there to bring you to know the love of God.

But do not hold on to your sins. Verse 14 stands: "Ephraim provoked Him to anger most bitterly; therefore his Lord will leave the guilt of his bloodshed upon him." Despite God's faithfulness, Ephraim disregarded him — and as a result had to bear the penalty for their sins. It is a dangerous and frightening thing not to have an alien atonement for your sins. It is a frightening thing to stand before God without someone who is pleading for you, who is willing to take upon himself your sins and their guilt.

God is just — he stands for his holiness, and all your sins are against him, and all your sins will be dealt with. But God is also gracious. The Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of God and God the Son, took upon himself the entirety of God's wrath that every one of his elect deserves.

Some ex-evangelicals mock the crucifixion, saying all Christ had was a bad weekend — he died on Friday and rose on Sunday. The point they miss is that in those limited hours on the cross, Jesus bore the infinite displeasure of God. It was not a bad weekend. It was the infinite price for you. And God calls you to believe that Jesus did that for you. And if you are his, he will pursue you all the way to the end — even if he needs to take you through exile from all your comforts. He will, and the glory awaiting you at the end will be beyond imagining. If you are his, there is nothing you can do to separate yourself from his love.

So be wise: walk in that love right now instead of running away and going through unnecessary suffering. Our Lord is just; he is also gracious — and that is seen on the face of the Lord Jesus Christ.

Let us pray together. Father, thank you for the prophecy of Hosea. Thank you that in your kindness to us, you repeat yourself over and over because we need to hear it. Thank you that in judgment, there is grace — there is always a way back through repentance. We pray that we would indeed repent of our sins and faithfully follow You. We ask this in Jesus' name. Amen.
